basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Qadr — “Power”, “Decree”, “Value”: three converging senses in one word. It is the night when the Quran was revealed. The night when, says Tustarī, “mercy upon the servants was decreed.” But Qushayrī goes deeper: in the word qadr (قَدْر) he distinguishes two measures. “It is a night in which the worshippers find the measure of their own soul, and in which the gnostics (ʿārifūn, عَارِفُون) contemplate the measure of their Adored. And what a distance between finding a measure of self and contemplating a measure of the Beloved!” The word qadr thus turns upon itself: it says the value the soul comes to recognise in the vigil, and it says the greatness of the Lord that this same vigil allows to be glimpsed. For those who love, Qushayrī adds, it is a short night: they are in intimate conversation (musāmara, مُسَامَرَة) with the Beloved. The Prophet spent these nights in spiritual retreat (iʿtikāf, اعْتِكَاف), teaching this invocation: “My God, You are the One who pardons, and You love pardon — pardon me.”

versets.trad: Truly We sent it down in the Night of Power.
versets.commentaire: anzalnāhu (أَنزَلْنَاهُ): “We sent it down”. The pronoun hu refers to the Quran. According to several commentators, this verse names the first descent — when the Quran was conveyed in its entirety from the Lawḥ al-Maḥfūẓ (لَوْح المَحْفُوظ), the Preserved Tablet, to the heaven of this world (the House of Might, bayt al-ʿizza); only afterward was it revealed in fragments to the Prophet over twenty-three years. Qadr (قَدْر) carries several senses: divine decree, measure, value, power. The word covers all — it is the Night when God decrees, measures, and when His Power manifests itself to humanity.

mots.def: emphasis particle inna + suffix pronoun We.
anzala: to send down, to bring down; perfect 1st pers. plur. + masc. sing. suffix pronoun (referring to the Quran).
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 1st pers. plur.

mots.def: preposition: in, during.
layla: the night; in the construct genitive: “the Night of”.
qadr: measure, divine decree, value, power; all these senses converge in the Laylat al-Qadr.

versets.trad: And what shall apprise you of what the Night of Power is?
versets.commentaire: As everywhere the Quran uses this formula — mā adrāka (مَا أَدْرَاكَ) —, what follows is less a definition than a sign. The Night of Power exceeds human knowing. According to Sh., the rhetorical question underlines “the utter holiness of this night”, whose true knowledge belongs only to God. The verse leaves the sentence hanging; the next three will not explain it, but will speak its greatness.

mots.def: conjunction.
interrogative.
adrā: to make know; perfect + suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: interrogative relative.
layla: night.
see v. 1.

versets.ar: لَيْلَةُ الْقَدْرِ خَيْرٌ مِّنْ أَلْفِ شَهْرٍ
versets.tr: laylatu l-qadri khayrun min alfi shahr
versets.trad: The Night of Power is better than a thousand months!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī adds a precision that changes everything: “better than a thousand months in which there would be no Night of qadr.” The comparison is not between one night and any thousand months; it is between a night traversed by God and a whole duration from which God would be absent. The grace of one inhabited moment exceeds the age of a life full of itself. It is also, for the lovers, a night that passes without their noticing. Qushayrī cites these lines: “O night among the nights of all time — in which I saw my full moon meet my full moon — without dusk and without dawn — until she departed, still virgin, the prime of time.”

mots.def: layla: night.
see v. 1.
khayr: good, best; here comparative: better than.
preposition of comparison.
alf: a thousand; here more broadly, an immeasurable duration.
shahr: month; in the indefinite genitive.

versets.ar: تَنَزَّلُ الْمَلَائِكَةُ وَالرُّوحُ فِيهَا بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِم مِّن كُلِّ أَمْرٍ
versets.tr: tanazzalu l-malāʾikatu wa-r-rūḥu fīhā bi-idhni rabbihim min kulli amr
versets.trad: The angels and the Spirit descend therein by the leave of their Lord, with every decree.
versets.commentaire: al-malāʾika wa-r-rūḥ (المَلَائِكَة وَالرُّوح): the angels and the Spirit. Most commentators identify al-Rūḥ (الرُّوح) with the archangel Gabriel, named by a higher title, or with a still loftier class of angels. The night is traversed by unseen messengers. Qushayrī gives the verse a striking reading: min kulli amr salām can be heard as “with each angel commanded, a greeting” — and he glosses: “with each of them, My salutation upon My friends.” The descent of the angels would then be first of all the passage of a salutation God Himself addresses to His own. That night, the heavens open, and God — by the angels — calls by name each one who keeps vigil for Him.

mots.def: tanazzala: derived form of nazala, to descend in stages, to descend in numbers; in the imperfect.
mots.gram: imperfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing. (non-rational plural subject)

mots.def: malāʾika: plural of malak/malaʾak, angel; envoys of the subtle world.
rūḥ: spirit, breath; here the Spirit par excellence — most commonly identified with Gabriel, the angel of revelation.
preposition fī + feminine suffix pronoun: in it (the Night).
idhn: permission, leave; preposition bi- + construct noun.
rabb + plural suffix pronoun.
preposition min: here, “with”, “for”.
kull: every, all; in the construct genitive.
amr: command, decree, affair; what God has ordained.

versets.trad: Peace it is, until the break of dawn.
versets.commentaire: salām (سَلَام): peace — full, without lack. The Arabic syntax places the word first: salāmun hiya, “peace it is”, as if the night were first a quality and only then a time. Tustarī specifies what this peace means for the friends of God: “it is the safeguard that the moments (awqāt, أَوْقَات) of those who know Him will not be cut off (qaṭʿ, قَطْع).” The subtlest spiritual danger is precisely this: that high moments break — that vigil be interrupted by distraction, that the light of one hour fade before bearing its fruit. The Night of qadr carries the salutation that prevents that breaking. For the one who watches in her, nothing will be cut short: the grace will hold, until the rising of the dawn — and beyond.

mots.def: salām: peace, salutation, plenitude without lack; one of the Divine Names.
feminine personal pronoun: it (referring to the Night).
particle: until.
maṭlaʿ: the place or moment of rising (of a heavenly body); construct genitive.
fajr: dawn, the first light that cleaves the night. Same root family as falaq.
